s3.q: Maternal underweight before pregnancy (BMI <19.8 kg/m²) increases the risk of:
s3.explain: Maternal underweight before pregnancy and low pregnancy weight gain increase risk of low infant birth weight.

s3.q: Which of the following is a pre-pregnancy preparation recommendation?
options: Achieve and maintain healthy body weight
s3.explain: Pre-pregnancy preparation includes: achieving/maintaining healthy weight, balanced diet, physical activity, regular medical care, and managing chronic conditions.

s4.q: Which of the following is NOT a component of nutritional care during pregnancy?
s4.explain: The four components are: (1) Nutritional Assessment, (2) Nutrition Education, (3) Nutrition Supplementation (iron, folate, calcium), (4) Correction of Deficiencies.

s4.q: Which supplementation is included as part of routine nutritional care during pregnancy?
s4.explain: Nutrition supplementation includes iron, folate, and calcium as key components.

It applies only to specific populations

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s5.q: What method is used for dietary history assessment in pregnancy?
s5.explain: The 24-hour recall method is used for semi-quantitative/qualitative analysis to identify expected major deficiencies.

s5.q: According to Egyptian guidelines, anemia in pregnancy is defined as hemoglobin less than:
s5.explain: By Egyptian guidelines, anemia in pregnancy is defined as Hb <11 g/dL in the first trimester.

s5.q: When should Vitamin D levels be measured in pregnancy?
options: Only in women with obesity, minimal sun exposure, malabsorption, or dark skin
s5.explain: Routine broad-based screening for vitamin D is not necessary. Measure in: obesity, minimal sun exposure, malabsorption (celiac, IBD), vegan diet, dark skin.

s6.q: For a normal weight woman (BMI 18.5-24.9), what is the mean recommended rate of gestational weight gain per week?
s6.explain: The mean rate for normal weight women (BMI 18.5-24.9) is 0.42 kg/week.

s6.q: Moderate anemia in pregnancy is defined as hemoglobin between:
s6.explain: Moderate anemia: Hb 7-9.9 g/dL. Mild: 10-10.9 g/dL. Severe: <7 g/dL.

s7.q: Which of the following is a key component of healthy eating during pregnancy?
options: Limit added sugars, saturated fat, and sodium
s7.explain: Key components include limiting added sugars, saturated fat, and sodium, appropriate supplementation, appropriate weight gain, avoiding alcohol, limiting caffeine to <200-300 mg/day, and safe food handling.

s7.q: What is the recommended caffeine limit during pregnancy?
s7.explain: Caffeine should be limited to <200-300 mg/day during pregnancy.

s8.q: How many servings of vegetables per day are recommended in the Food Guide Pyramid during pregnancy?
s8.explain: The Food Guide Pyramid recommends 3-5 servings of vegetables and 3-4 servings of fruit per day.

s8.q: How many servings per day from the milk group are recommended during pregnancy?
s8.explain: The milk group (milk, yogurt, cheese) recommends 4 servings per day during pregnancy.

s9.q: What is the routine iron supplementation recommendation for pregnant women after 3 months?
options: 220 mg ferrous fumarate/sulphate + folic acid once daily
330 mg ferrous sulphate three times daily
s9.explain: All pregnant mothers (after 3 months) should receive 220 mg ferrous fumarate/sulphate + folic acid once daily.

s9.q: What is the folic acid dose for women at high risk of NTD (previous NTD or NTD in either parent)?
options: 4 mg once daily prior to conception through first 12 weeks
s9.explain: High-risk women (previous NTD) should receive 4 mg folic acid once daily from at least 1 month prior to conception through first 12 weeks.

s9.q: Why should vitamin A supplementation be avoided during pregnancy?
options: It may be teratogenic in doses above 700 micrograms
It increases the risk of gestational diabetes
s9.explain: Vitamin A intake above 700 micrograms might be teratogenic and should be avoided during pregnancy.

s10.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s10.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s10.q: What is the curative dose of ferrous fumarate for iron deficiency anemia in pregnancy?
s10.explain: Curative management of iron deficiency anemia: ferrous fumarate or sulphate 200 mg 3 times daily.

s10.q: Which of the following is an indication for IV iron administration in pregnancy?
options: Severe or symptomatic anemia, especially later in pregnancy
s10.explain: IV iron is indicated: beyond 1st trimester and cannot tolerate oral iron, severe/symptomatic anemia (especially later in pregnancy), oral iron ineffective.

s10.q: Within what time frame should Hb increase by ≥1 g/dL after starting iron therapy?
s10.explain: Hb should increase by ≥1 g/dL within 2-3 weeks of starting iron therapy.
